reasoning:
The ECB is currently actively shrinking its balance sheet through quantitative tightening (APP and PEPP run-offs, TLTRO maturing), which exerts a strong structural downward bias on total assets. Recent FRED data shows that after an unexplained large jump in early October 2025 (to 6,209,218), the balance sheet is once again drifting downward weekly (sitting at 6,195,379 on Oct 24). 
For the short-term horizon (Nov 2), we are confident this steady intramonth decline will keep levels below the Oct 26 reference. However, uncertainty increases rapidly over the medium (Nov 25) and longer term (Jan 24). The first quarter frequently brings early-year asset bumps (as seen in Jan 2025 and Apr 2025), which increases the variance of the path. Most crucially, statements in late October from European policymakers (such as the DNB) specify that 'at some point, the ECB balance sheet will have to grow again' to meet structural liquidity demands. Given the ambiguity of when exactly the ECB will transition from active contraction to steady/growing structural liquidity provisions, the 3-month forecast is highly uncertain. Following the prompt's instruction, and reflecting the genuine uncertainty about this impending structural pivot, I have strictly increased uncertainty (shifted probabilities closer to 0.5) as the forecast horizon extends.
